categories.title: Phishing Attack (Fake Bank / KYC / OTP Scam)
categories.description: A cybercrime where a fraudster sends fake emails, SMS, WhatsApp messages, or creates fake websites impersonating legitimate banks, government bodies, or payment platforms to trick victims into revealing sensitive credentials such as OTPs, passwords, card numbers, or net banking login details. The goal is to gain unauthorized access to financial accounts.
categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to phishing via LINKS (email/SMS). Differentiator: victim clicked a fake bank/KYC link or was called asking for OTP. If UPI app was used → CT004. If email identity was forged → CT010.

categories.title: UPI Fraud / QR Code Scam

categories.title: Fake Loan App / Instant Loan Scam
categories.description: A cybercrime where fraudsters operate illegal mobile lending apps or fake websites offering instant personal loans with minimal documentation. Victims are lured with promises of quick approval but are then subjected to excessive interest rates, hidden charges, harassment for repayment even on paid loans, and blackmail using personal data (contacts, photos) accessed by the malicious app. Many such apps operate without RBI authorization.

categories.title: Investment Scam / Crypto Scam
categories.description: A cybercrime where fraudsters lure victims with promises of high returns on investments through fake trading platforms, cryptocurrency schemes, Ponzi schemes, or fraudulent stock/forex trading apps. Victims are convinced to invest increasing amounts of money, shown fake profits on bogus dashboards, and then denied withdrawal. categories.title: Cyber Bullying / Online Harassment
categories.description: Cyber bullying and online harassment refer to the deliberate, repeated, and hostile use of digital platforms — including social media, messaging apps, online gaming, email, and forums — to intimidate, threaten, humiliate, embarrass, or emotionally harm an individual. Unlike physical bullying, cyber bullying is pervasive, can occur 24/7, leaves a permanent digital trail, and can involve a wide audience. It encompasses a broad range of behaviors including sending threatening messages, spreading rumors, public shaming, doxxing (publishing private information), exclusion from online groups, morphing and sharing images, sexting under pressure, cyberstalking, impersonation to harm, and coordinated group harassment campaigns. Victims include adults, teenagers, and children — with minors being particularly vulnerable. Perpetrators may be known individuals (classmates, ex-partners, colleagues) or anonymous online actors.

categories.title: Cyber Stalking
categories.description: Cyber stalking is the use of the internet, electronic communication, social media, GPS tracking, spyware, or any digital technology to repeatedly monitor, follow, contact, threaten, or harass an individual — causing fear, distress, or emotional harm. Unlike cyberbullying which may be public, cyber stalking often involves covert surveillance and obsessive monitoring of a victim's online activity, location, communications, and daily life. It commonly occurs in the context of domestic relationships, failed romances, workplace conflicts, or obsessive fixation on a stranger or celebrity. Cyber stalking may escalate into physical stalking, which makes it a safety-critical offense. It encompasses monitoring social media silently, tracking real-time location via shared apps or hidden spyware, sending repeated unwanted messages after being asked to stop, creating fake accounts to watch the victim, hacking into victim's accounts to read private messages, intercepting emails or calls, contacting the victim's family, friends, or employer, sending unsolicited gifts with tracking capability, and gathering information to enable physical confrontation.

categories.title: Online Defamation
categories.description: Online defamation refers to the publication of false statements of fact about a person through digital platforms — including social media, websites, blogs, forums, review sites, messaging apps, and any internet-based medium — that damages or is intended to damage the reputation, dignity, or social standing of that individual. Under Indian law, defamation can be both a civil wrong (actionable in civil court for damages) and a criminal offence (punishable under IPC Section 499/500). Online defamation is particularly dangerous because digital content spreads rapidly, reaches a massive audience instantly, is difficult to erase completely, and can cause lasting, irreversible damage to personal reputation, career, business, relationships, and mental health. It includes fake reviews, false allegations, fabricated screenshots, impersonation with defamatory content, posting private facts out of context, morphed images with false captions, fake news articles, viral WhatsApp forwards containing lies, defamatory YouTube videos, Instagram posts, Facebook posts, Twitter posts, and coordinated defamation campaigns. The line between opinion/criticism and defamation is legally important — truth is an absolute defense in Indian law, and fair comment on matters of public interest may be protected.

ipc.title: Defamation (Definition)
ipc.penalty: Defines the offence — making or publishing any imputation concerning a person, intending to harm or knowing it will harm the reputation of such person. Includes words, signs, visible representations. Online posts, comments, videos, and messages fall within this definition.

Censure by a person in authority over another in good faith
Complaint made in good faith to authority

categories.title: Hate Speech / Online Abuse
categories.description: Hate speech and online abuse refer to the use of digital platforms — including social media, messaging apps, websites, forums, livestreams, and any online medium — to promote hatred, violence, discrimination, or hostility against individuals or groups based on protected characteristics such as religion, race, caste, ethnicity, nationality, gender, sexual orientation, language, or place of birth. In India, hate speech is a criminal offence governed by multiple IPC sections, and is also regulated by IT Rules 2021 which mandate platforms to take down hate speech content. Online abuse in this category includes incitement to violence against communities, communal hatred posts, caste-based slurs and abuse, anti-national speech used to target individuals, religious hate content, doxxing of minorities, coordinated harassment of marginalized communities, misogynistic abuse targeting women, LGBTQ+ targeted abuse, regional and linguistic discrimination, and the spread of hateful propaganda and disinformation designed to polarize communities. The challenge in this domain is distinguishing protected free speech and legitimate criticism from unlawful incitement to hate — which requires assessment of context, intent, and likely effect. India's constitutional framework permits reasonable restrictions on free speech in the interests of public order, decency, and morality.

categories.title: Malware Attack / Virus / Ransomware
categories.description: A malware attack is the deliberate deployment of malicious software — including viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, adware, rootkits, keyloggers, botnets, cryptojackers, wipers, and fileless malware — into a target's computer, mobile device, server, or network without authorization, with the intent to damage, disrupt, steal data, spy, extort money, or gain unauthorized control. In India, malware attacks have grown exponentially targeting individuals, businesses, hospitals, government institutions, banks, and critical infrastructure. Ransomware attacks specifically encrypt victim's files and demand cryptocurrency ransom for decryption keys. They frequently target SMEs, hospitals, and government departments. Malware is typically delivered through phishing emails, malicious attachments, infected USB drives, drive-by downloads on compromised websites, fake app stores, social engineering, and supply chain attacks. The damage from malware attacks includes complete data loss, financial extortion, exposure of sensitive personal and business data, system downtime, reputational harm, and in critical infrastructure attacks — threats to national security and public safety. India's IT Act 2000 and its amendments provide criminal penalties for malware creation, distribution, and deployment, with Section 66F covering the most severe cases of cyber terrorism through malware targeting critical infrastructure.

categories.title: Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS Attack)
categories.description: A Denial of Service (DoS) attack is an attempt to make a computer, server, website, network, or digital service unavailable to its intended users by overwhelming it with illegitimate traffic, requests, or malformed data that exhaust system resources. A Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack scales this by using thousands or millions of compromised computers (a botnet) simultaneously, making it vastly more powerful and harder to block. In India, DoS and DDoS attacks represent a major cybersecurity threat targeting e-commerce platforms, banking systems, government websites, news portals, fintech companies, gaming servers, healthcare institutions, and political/social targets. Attack motivations include financial extortion (pay or we will keep attacking), hacktivism (political protest), competitive sabotage (crippling a business rival's operations), targeted revenge, state-sponsored attacks on critical infrastructure, and as a distraction cover for data theft or other simultaneous cyber attacks. Attack types include volumetric attacks (UDP/ICMP flood, DNS amplification), protocol attacks (SYN flood, Ping of Death, Smurf attack), and application layer attacks (HTTP flood, Slowloris, slow-speed attacks targeting web application logic). Organizations most vulnerable include SMEs without DDoS protection, government websites, banking portals, online retailers during peak seasons, gaming platforms, and media/news organizations. The financial and reputational damage from sustained DDoS attacks can be enormous — including lost revenue, breach of SLA contracts, regulatory scrutiny, and long-term loss of customer trust.
categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to server/website flooding attacks. Differentiator: service became unavailable due to traffic flood. If website was defaced → CT022. If data was stolen → CT021.

categories.title: Data Breach / Unauthorized Data Access
categories.description: A data breach is a security incident in which confidential, sensitive, protected, or personal data is accessed, disclosed, copied, stolen, transmitted, or used by an unauthorized individual, either through external hacking, insider threat, accidental exposure, or negligent data handling practices. In India, data breaches have become increasingly frequent affecting banks, healthcare institutions, government databases, e-commerce platforms, telecom companies, educational institutions, and individual users. Breaches can expose personally identifiable information (PII) including Aadhaar numbers, PAN cards, financial account details, medical records, biometric data, login credentials, and private communications. The consequences of a data breach include financial fraud, identity theft, reputational damage, regulatory fines, legal liability, and in sensitive cases — threats to national security. India's legal framework for data breach response is now significantly strengthened by the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act), which mandates breach notification to the Data Protection Board (DPB) and to affected individuals (Data Principals). IT Act Sections 43, 43A, 66, and 72A provide criminal and civil remedies. Unauthorized data access covers both external attacks (hacking, SQL injection, credential stuffing, API abuse, insider theft) and internal data leaks (employee misuse, accidental public exposure, misconfigured cloud storage). Organizations processing large amounts of personal data now have significantly heightened legal obligations under the DPDP Act 2023.

categories.title: Website Defacement / Hacking
categories.description: Website defacement is a form of cyber attack where an unauthorized attacker modifies the visual appearance, content, or functionality of a website without the owner's permission — typically replacing the homepage with the attacker's message, political propaganda, hacktivist statements, religious content, national flags, or simply demonstrating hacking capability. Website hacking is the broader category encompassing all unauthorized access, modification, exploitation, or control of websites and web applications — including defacement, backdoor installation, data theft, carding infrastructure setup, malware distribution, SEO poisoning, phishing page hosting, cryptojacking, and turning the site into a botnet command-and-control server. In India, website defacement attacks frequently target government websites, political party sites, educational institutions, media portals, financial services, and high-profile corporate websites. Attack methods include SQL injection (exploiting database queries), Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Remote File Inclusion (RFI), Local File Inclusion (LFI), XML External Entity attacks (XXE), Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), insecure file upload exploitation, credential brute-forcing, outdated CMS exploitation (WordPress, Joomla, Drupal), unpatched plugin vulnerabilities, weak FTP/SSH credentials, subdomain takeover, DNS hijacking, and supply chain compromise of web hosting infrastructure. Pakistani hacktivist groups (Team Insane PK, Mysterious Team), Chinese state-sponsored actors, and domestic cybercriminals frequently target Indian websites. Defacement of government websites (NIC-hosted, gov.in domains) is treated with particular seriousness and investigated by CERT-In and cyber police.

categories.title: Personal Data Leak / Privacy Violation
categories.description: A personal data leak or privacy violation occurs when an individual's private, sensitive, or personally identifiable information is accessed, disclosed, published, shared, transmitted, or used without their knowledge or consent — by another individual, a company, a platform, a government body, or a malicious actor. Unlike organizational data breaches (CT021), this category focuses on violations from the individual victim's perspective — including sharing of private photos or videos without consent, leaking Aadhaar/PAN/financial details, exposing location data without permission, revealing medical or mental health records, disclosing sexual orientation, sharing private communications, and misuse of data collected through apps, websites, or services. India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act) has fundamentally transformed the privacy landscape, establishing clear rights for individuals (Data Principals) and obligations for organizations (Data Fiduciaries). This includes the right to know what data is collected, the right to correction and erasure, the right to nominate, the right to grievance redressal, and protection from processing without valid consent or legitimate use. The IT Act 2000 provides additional protections through Sections 43A (corporate negligence), 66E (privacy violation — images), 72 (breach of confidentiality), and 72A (contractual breach of privacy). Privacy violations can cause severe psychological harm, relationship damage, professional consequences, safety risks, and financial harm. The overlap between privacy violation and other cybercrimes (stalking, defamation, harassment, sextortion) means many victims need multi-faceted legal remedies.

categories.title: Unauthorized Data Sharing by Company
categories.description: Unauthorized data sharing by a company refers to a Data Fiduciary (any organization processing personal data) sharing, selling, disclosing, or transmitting personal data of individuals (Data Principals) to third parties without valid consent, beyond the scope of stated purpose, in violation of their own privacy policy, without legal basis, or in contravention of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 and IT Act provisions. This includes selling user data to advertisers, sharing customer data with business partners without disclosure, disclosing employee data without authorization, sharing sensitive personal data with foreign entities in violation of data localization requirements, data brokers aggregating and selling personal profiles, fintech companies sharing financial data with affiliates, healthcare companies sharing patient data with insurance companies, telecom operators selling call records, and apps sharing behavioral and location data to third-party SDKs without user knowledge. The DPDP Act 2023 has dramatically strengthened protections — requiring consent to be free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous, with clear affirmative action. Processing beyond consent or without legitimate legal basis now attracts penalties up to ₹250 crore. Organizations must maintain purpose limitation (data only used for stated purpose), data minimization (only collect what is needed), storage limitation (not retain beyond purpose), and must have a lawful basis for every processing activity. The challenge for victims is often not knowing their data is being shared — organizations may hide data sharing in lengthy, opaque privacy policies or use dark patterns to obtain uninformed consent.

categories.title: Fake E-commerce Website Scam
categories.description: A fake e-commerce website scam involves fraudsters creating counterfeit online shopping websites that impersonate legitimate retailers, luxury brands, or popular platforms — or setting up entirely fictitious online stores — to deceive consumers into paying for goods that are never delivered, are counterfeit, or are drastically different from what was advertised. These fake websites are designed with professional-looking layouts, fake product images stolen from genuine retailers, fabricated customer reviews, fake trust badges, and attractive heavily discounted prices to lure victims. Common tactics include creating lookalike domains (amazan.in, flipkart-sale.com, myntra-sale.co.in), running Facebook/Instagram/Google ads for non-existent sales, impersonating government surplus or auction websites, creating fake 'going out of business' sales, festival sale scams (Diwali, Holi, Independence Day specials), fake designer/luxury brand websites selling counterfeits at 'discounted' prices, military/defence equipment surplus scams, fake agricultural equipment stores, animal/pet purchase scams, and fake ticket booking platforms. Victims pay via UPI, debit/credit card, or net banking and never receive goods, or receive completely different worthless items. Repeat victimization is common as victims may try to get refunds through fake customer care numbers that extract more money. India's expanding e-commerce market — projected to reach $120 billion by 2026 — and the explosion of social media advertising have made this one of the fastest-growing consumer cybercrimes.

categories.title: Online Job Fraud / Work From Home Scam
categories.description: Online job fraud and work-from-home scams are a rapidly growing category of cyber fraud in India that exploit job seekers' desperation, financial insecurity, and aspirations for flexible income. These scams take many forms — ranging from fake job offers from impersonated companies, advance fee frauds where registration or training fees are collected before disappearing, task-based earning scams where victims earn small amounts initially before being defrauded of large investments, part-time data entry or form-filling scams, fake HR calls collecting personal documents, fake offer letter scams collecting processing fees, fake internship programs, fake freelance marketplace scams, fake YouTube/Instagram like-and-earn schemes, fake crypto trading job scams, and fake call center recruitment scams. The COVID-19 pandemic dramatically accelerated work-from-home fraud as millions of job seekers moved online. Fraudsters specifically target students, housewives, recently unemployed individuals, recent graduates, and people in financial distress. Fake job portals impersonate Naukri.com, LinkedIn, Indeed, Monster.com, and even legitimate companies like TCS, Infosys, Amazon, Google, and various MNCs. The psychological manipulation used includes creating artificial urgency, showing initial small earnings to build trust before demanding larger investments, impersonating HR managers of reputed companies, and exploiting victims' need for income. Task-based scams (YouTube likes, Telegram group tasks, hotel/restaurant ratings) are particularly sophisticated — victims earn and withdraw small amounts initially, are then asked to invest larger amounts for 'premium tasks' or 'VIP membership', and lose everything when they try to withdraw.

categories.title: Courier / Parcel Scam
categories.description: The Courier/Parcel Scam is a sophisticated cybercrime in which fraudsters impersonate courier companies (FedEx, DHL, Blue Dart, India Post, DTDC), customs officials, narcotics control bureau officers, CBI agents, or police to threaten and extort money from victims. The scam takes two primary forms: (1) The Customs/Narcotics Threat Variant — victim receives a call claiming a parcel containing drugs, contraband, counterfeit currency, or illegal items was dispatched in their name or intercepted at customs. The fraudster impersonating an official demands immediate payment to avoid arrest, FIR, or legal action. This is amplified with 'digital arrest' threats where victims are kept on video call for hours or days while being psychologically manipulated. (2) The Undelivered Parcel/Prize Variant — victim is told a valuable package (prize, inheritance, lottery, gift from abroad) is stuck at customs and requires payment of customs duty, release fee, or taxes for delivery. Both variants use impersonation of law enforcement and government agencies, manufactured urgency, threats of arrest/legal consequences, and high psychological pressure. The 'Digital Arrest' phenomenon specifically became a major concern in India in 2024-2025, where victims are kept on video calls by fraudsters dressed as police/customs officers in fake 'office' settings, terrorizing them for extended periods. Prime Minister Modi specifically addressed digital arrest scams in his Mann Ki Baat address in October 2024. Victims lose anywhere from ₹10,000 to several crores.
keywords: case band karne ke liye paise
tax pay karo parcel lene ke liye
stayed at home due to fraud call
cbi ncb ed fake courier arrest
delivery held pay fee fraud call
primary_identifiers: cbi ncb ed fake courier arrest
categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to courier parcel + fake arrest threat calls. Differentiator: call mentioned a parcel in victim's name containing illegal items + demanded money to avoid arrest.

Account frozen after winning - won't let me withdraw

categories.description: Platform suspended your account for Terms of Service (ToS) violation. This is the platform's policy enforcement - NOT a criminal act. Platforms have the legal right to suspend accounts violating their rules.

A hacker - unauthorized person changed my password and locked me out

immediate_actions: 📧 Appeal to Platform: Most platforms have appeal process

categories.legal_summary: Platforms (Instagram, Facebook, etc.) are private companies. They can refuse service to anyone for ToS violations. Courts rarely interfere unless discrimination is proven.

explanation: Accidentally sending money to wrong UPI ID / account is USER ERROR, not cyber crime.
The recipient did not steal or hack - you voluntarily transferred.
This is a CIVIL matter (contract/unjust enrichment), not criminal.
why_not_crime: ✗ You willingly initiated the transaction
✗ No fraud, deception, or hacking occurred
✗ Recipient didn't trick you - you made typing error
✗ This falls under civil law (unjust enrichment), not cyber crime law

your_options: 📞 Call Your Bank IMMEDIATELY: Request transaction reversal (works if within 24 hours sometimes)

📲 Contact Recipient: If you have their number, request refund politely (save chat proof)
👮 Police Complaint: Can file, but police rarely act on accidental transfers
⚖️ Civil Court: File civil suit for recovery (if amount is large, >Rs. 50,000)
💸 Banking Ombudsman: File complaint if bank refuses to help

bank_reversal_reality.upi_specifics: UPI transfers are INSTANT and FINAL. Reversal needs recipient's consent.

if_user_mistake.police_approach: Most police stations will say: 'This is civil matter, not crime. Go to civil court.' Police MAY help if amount is very large (₹1 lakh+) and recipient is uncooperative.

civil_court_option.court_fees: Based on amount (e.g., Rs. 5,000 for Rs. 1 lakh claim)

if_user_mistake.prevention_tip: Always verify UPI ID / account number TWICE before sending. Use UPI beneficiary name verification.

categories.title: Blocked/Unfriended on Social Media by Another User

not_crime_data.scenario_name: Blocked/Unfriended on Social Media by Another User
not_crime_data.scenario_hindi: Kisi ne social media pe block kar diya / unfriend kar diya

detection_logic.no_question_needed: This is clearly not a crime - respond immediately

explanation: Someone blocking/unfriending you on social media is NOT a cyber crime.
People have the fundamental right to choose who they interact with.
Blocking is a privacy feature - it's not illegal.
why_not_crime: ✗ Everyone has right to block anyone (freedom of association)
✗ No theft, fraud, or illegal access occurred
✗ Social media interaction is voluntary, not mandatory
✗ Blocking is a platform feature for user safety/privacy
response.legal_reality: Indian law does NOT recognize 'right to be friends' or 'right to access someone's social media'. Blocking is legal and within user's rights.
response.exceptions: ONLY a crime if there was harassment, threats, or blackmail BEFORE the blocking. Otherwise, blocking itself is legal.
what_to_do: Do NOT create fake accounts to contact them (that's harassment)
Do NOT send messages through friends (that's stalking behavior)
If there's a genuine issue, resolve through mutual friends/family
response.if_harassment_involved: If they blocked you AFTER you harassed/threatened them, YOU may be committing a crime (cyber stalking, harassment). If they harassed YOU and then blocked, that harassment may be a crime - but blocking itself isn't.
